topic.what: data recovery attack exploiting subspace leakage in SVD-based matrix transmission protocol for secure transformer inference
topic.how: exploits leaked orthogonal matrices U and H to recover masking matrix R, then reconstructs private inputs via R̃ = -UṼDH̃
topic.why_matters: exposes fundamental privacy risk where encrypting only singular values while transmitting orthogonal matrices in plaintext is insufficient for privacy protection

characteristics.what_it_measures: ratio of Frobenius norm difference between recovered and original input to original input norm
characteristics.evidence: Table I shows RRE across RTE, SST-2, QNLI datasets consistently ~0.036 regardless of masking scale

characteristics.what_it_measures: ratio of Frobenius norm difference between recovered and original input to original input norm
characteristics.evidence: Table I shows CIFAR-100 has higher RRE due to smaller input dimensionality

characteristics.what_it_measures: upper bound on expected relative recovery error derived analytically
characteristics.evidence: Theorem 1 proves E[∥Ā - A∥F / ∥A∥F] ≤ 1/√n where n is input dimension
characteristics.context: theoretical guarantee that error vanishes as input dimensionality increases

characteristics.what_it_measures: dependency of attack effectiveness on input dimensionality n
characteristics.evidence: CIFAR-100 (smaller) has higher RRE than language datasets (larger); theorem confirms error vanishes with increasing n

characteristics.what_it_measures: attack robustness across different masking scales η
characteristics.vs_baseline: attacks succeeds regardless of masking scale
characteristics.evidence: Table I shows consistent RRE values (0.034-0.037 for language, 0.174-0.180 for CIFAR) across all tested η values
characteristics.context: recovery depends on leaked singular subspaces, not mask magnitude

characteristics.what_itasures: bandwidth reduction claimed by Euston protocol
characteristics.vs_baseline: vs original m×n encrypted values reduced to m values
characteristics.evidence: From original Euston paper (IEEE S&P 2026): m×n values reduced to m values for encryption and transmission

requires: access to orthogonal matrices U and H

novelty: first data recovery attack against Euston SVD-based matrix transmission protocol
theoretical analysis of relative recovery error bound
demonstrates fundamental privacy insufficiency of encrypting only low-rank representations
empirical validation across text and image domains
open_problems: designing secure alternatives to SVD-based matrix transmission
protecting orthogonal matrix information in secure inference
formal security proofs for privacy-preserving matrix operations
extending analysis to other compression-based secure inference protocols
